zh.lifeSummary: 當Richard FitzGilbert de Clare於1030年8月10日出生在法國諾門迪艾布利奧安時，他的父親Gilbert de Brionne comte d'Eu et de Brionne31歲，母親Gunnora d' Aunou31歲。他和Rohese Giffard至少有7個兒子和4個女兒。他於1066年登記服兵役。他去世於1091年3月15日在英國亨廷格多史爾聖 辛茲享年60歲，埋葬在英國亨廷格多史爾聖 辛茲。

zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当Richard FitzGilbert de Clare于1030年8月10日出生在法国諾門迪艾布利奧安时，他的父亲Gilbert de Brionne comte d'Eu et de Brionne31岁，母亲Gunnora d' Aunou31岁。他和Rohese Giffard至少有7个儿子和4个女儿。他于1066年登记服兵役。他去世于1091年3月15日在英国亨廷格多史爾聖 辛茲享年60岁，埋葬在英国亨廷格多史爾聖 辛茲。

nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.
Indian: variant of   Dey  .

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Clare
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Irish (Clare, Wicklow, and Wexford): habitational name from Clare in Suffolk (an eastern county of England), the center of a major Norman baronial estate since the Norman Conquest. The English placename probably originated as a Celtic river name meaning ‘bright, gentle, or warm’. The surname is not from the Irish county name Clare, which is either from the surname or from a place called with Gaelic clár ‘plank bridge’. Another Norman surname in the southeast of Ireland was de Clere, from Clères in Seine-Maritime (France), named from the local river.

English and French: from the Middle English, Old French female personal name Cla(i)re (from Latin Clara, from clarus ‘clear, bright, famous’) which achieved some popularity, greater elsewhere in Europe than in England, through the fame of Saint Clare of Assisi (see Chiara ). The English surname seems to have been interchangeable with Clear 1.

English: occupational name from an agent derivative of Middle English cley ‘clay’. The work of medieval clayers involved plastering a framework of interwoven twigs with mud to produce wattle-and-daub work.

variants.name: Claire
meanings: Irish: variant of   Clare  .
Variant of   Clair  , an altered form of German   Klare  .
French: variant of   Clair  .

variants.name: Chiara
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Marco, Amedeo, Angelo, Gino, Rocco.

Italian: from the female personal name Chiara or Clara, a name popularized in the medieval period by the cult of Saint Clare of Assisi (c. 1193–1253); she was an associate of Saint Francis of Assisi and founded the order of nuns known as the Poor Clares. See also Chiaro .

variants.name: Claar
meanings: German: variant of Klaar (and, in North America, an altered form of this), a nickname from Middle High German, Middle Low German klar ‘pure, pretty, sensible’, or a habitational name from a minor place so named near Braunschweig.
Possibly also an Americanized form of Dutch Klaar: from a vernacular form of the female personal name Clara (see Clare 2).

variants.name: Clarkin
meanings: English (Lancashire and Cheshire): from a pet form of the personal name Clare (compare Clare and Clear ), which is both a masculine and a female personal name in Middle English.
Irish (Monaghan): variant of   Clerkin  .

variants.name: Clear
meanings: English (southeastern England, of Norman origin): from Clere, the French popular form of the personal name Clare 2, and possibly interchangeable with it or confused with it.

English (southeastern): probably a habitational name from clere, a component of several placenames in north Hampshire (Highclere, Burghclere, Kingsclere). This is probably from a Celtic stream name meaning ‘bright’ (cognate with Latin clarus ‘clear, bright’).
Americanized form (translation into English) of German   Klar   1.

nameMeanings.name: Richard
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rikert
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rickert
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

historicEvents.title: England Divided
historicEvents.description: King Canute died in 1035 and England was divided between his three sons. His son Sweyn received Norway, Harthacanute received Denmark, and Harold Harefoot received North England.

historicEvents.title: Battle of Hastings
historicEvents.description: Battle of Hastings was fought between the Norman-French army and the battle-wary English army on October 14, 1066. Duke William of Normandy believed he was the rightful king rather than Harold, who had been crowned king several months earlier. Harold was killed in battle and William was crowned king on Christmas Day 1066.

historicEvents.title: Domesday Book
historicEvents.description: Domesday Book is Britain's earliest extant public record with information containing land surveys and landholdings commissioned by William I. The book was completed in 1086.

en.lifeSummary: When Richard FitzGilbert de Clare was born on 10 August 1030, in Brionne, Eure, Normandy, France, his father, Gilbert de Brionne comte d'Eu et de Brionne, was 31 and his mother, Gunnora d' Aunou, was 31. He had at least 7 sons and 4 daughters with Rohese Giffard. He registered for military service in 1066. He died on 15 March 1091, in St Neots, Huntingdonshire, England, at the age of 60, and was buried in St Neots, Huntingdonshire, England.

fr.Chepstow, Monmouthshire, Wales: Chepstow, Monmouthshire, Pays de Galles, Royaume-Uni
fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Richard FitzGilbert de Clare est né le 10 août 1030, à Brionne, Eure, Normandie, France, son père, Gilbert de Brionne comte d'Eu et de Brionne, avait 31 ans et sa mère, Gunnora d' Aunou, avait 31 ans. Il a eu au moins 7 fils et 4 filles avec Rohese Giffard. Il s'est inscrite au service militaire en 1066. Il est décédé le 15 mars 1091, à St Neots, Huntingdonshire, Angleterre, à l’âge de 60 ans, et a été enterré à St Neots, Huntingdonshire, Angleterre.

de.lifeSummary: Als Richard FitzGilbert de Clare am 10. August 1030 in Brionne, Eure, Normandie, Frankreich geboren wurde, war sein Vater Gilbert de Brionne comte d'Eu et de Brionne 31 und seine Mutter Gunnora d' Aunou 31. Er hatte mit Rohese Giffard mindestens 7 Söhne und 4 Töchter. Er hat sich 1066 zum Militärdienst angemeldet. Er starb am 15. März 1091 im Alter von 60 Jahren in St Neots, Huntingdonshire, England und wurde in St Neots, Huntingdonshire, England begraben.

it.lifeSummary: Quando Richard FitzGilbert de Clare è nato il il 10 agosto 1030 nel Brionne, Eure, Normandie, Francia, il suo padre, Gilbert de Brionne comte d'Eu et de Brionne, aveva 31 anni e il suo madre, Gunnora d' Aunou, aveva 31 anni. Lui avuto almeno 7 figli e 4 figlie con Rohese Giffard. Si è arruolato nell'esercito nel 1066. Morì il 15 marzo 1091, nel St Neots, Huntingdonshire, England, all'età di 60 anni, e fu sepolto a St Neots, Huntingdonshire, England.

ja.lifeSummary: Richard FitzGilbert de Clareが1030年8月10日にフランスノーマンディエウアブリオンで生まれた時，父Gilbert de Brionne comte d'Eu et de Brionneは31歳，母Gunnora d' Aunouは31歳であった。彼はRohese Giffardとの間に少なくとも7人の息子と4人の娘をもうけた。彼は1066年に兵役登録をした。彼は1091年3月15日にイギリスハンティングドンシュアイアスト ネオツで60歳で死去し，イギリスハンティングドンシュアイアスト ネオツにて埋葬された。

ko.lifeSummary: Richard FitzGilbert de Clare(이)가 1030년 8월 10일 프랑스 노먼디 우르 브리오니에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Gilbert de Brionne comte d'Eu et de Brionne(은)는 31세였고, 그의 어머니 Gunnora d' Aunou(은)는 31세였다. 그는 Rohese Giffard(와)과 적어도 일곱 명의 아들과 네 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 1066년에 군복무를 위해 등록했다. 그는 1091년 3월 15일 영국 헌틴그도실르 스트 니오츠에서 60세의 나이로 사망했으며 영국 헌틴그도실르 스트 니오츠에 묻혔다.

pt.10 August 1030–15 March 1091: 10 de agosto de 1030–15 de março de 1091
pt.lifeSummary: Quando Richard FitzGilbert de Clare nasceu em 10 de agosto de 1030, em Brionne, Eure, Normandie, França, seu pai, Gilbert de Brionne comte d'Eu et de Brionne, tinha 31 anos e sua mãe, Gunnora d' Aunou, tinha 31 anos. Ele teve pelo menos 7 filhos e 4 filhas com Rohese Giffard. Ele alistou-se no serviço militar em 1066. Ele faleceu em 15 de março de 1091, em St Neots, Huntingdonshire, Inglaterra, com 60 anos, e foi sepultado em St Neots, Huntingdonshire, Inglaterra.

ro.Chepstow, Monmouthshire, Wales: Chepstow, Monmouthshire, Țara Galilor, United Kingdom

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Richard FitzGilbert de Clare родился 10 августа 1030 г. в местности брионн, эур, норманди, Франция, его отцу по имени Gilbert de Brionne comte d'Eu et de Brionne было 31 год, а матери, которую звали Gunnora d' Aunou, 31. У него было по крайней мере 7 сыновей и 4 дочери от Rohese Giffard. В 1066 году он поступил на военную службу. Он умер 15 марта 1091 г. в местности ст неоц, хантингдоншир, Англия в возрасте 60 лет и был похоронен в местности ст неоц, хантингдоншир, Англия.

sm.From 1066 to 1090: mai le 1066 e o'o I le 1090

es.10 August 1030–15 March 1091: 10 de agosto de 1030–15 de marzo de 1091
es.lifeSummary: Cuando Richard FitzGilbert de Clare nació el 10 de agosto de 1030, en Brionne, Eure, Normandie, Francia, su papá, Gilbert de Brionne comte d'Eu et de Brionne, tenía 31 años y su mamá, Gunnora d' Aunou, tenía 31 años. Tuvo por lo menos 7 hijos y 4 hijas con Rohese Giffard. Se enlistó en el ejército en 1066. Murió el 15 de marzo de 1091, en St Neots, Huntingdonshire, Inglaterra, a la edad de 60 años, y fue sepultado en St Neots, Huntingdonshire, Inglaterra.
